metadata.name: Daily Reflection Tree
metadata.description: A deterministic end-of-day reflection tool walking three psychological axes.

axis1.internal: you owned your responses today — you stayed in the driver's seat even when outcomes were uncertain
axis1.external: circumstances felt bigger than your choices today — worth noticing where you did have agency

axis2.contribution: you gave without keeping score — your focus was on others' success, not your own ledger
axis2.entitlement: the internal scorecard was running today — what you gave versus what you felt you deserved

axis3.self: your frame was personal and close — useful for focus, but narrow for meaning
axis3.team: you held the team in mind alongside yourself — your concern extended to your immediate circle
axis3.others: you thought beyond the immediate — toward people your work serves, even ones you may never meet

nodes.text:
Good evening. Take a breath — today is almost done.

Let's spend a few minutes with it. Not to judge it. Just to see it clearly.
How would you describe your day overall?

options.label: Productive
Mixed
Tough
Frustrating

nodes.comment: Routes based on the opening tone of the day

nodes.text: You said '{A1_OPEN}'. When things went well today, what made it happen?

options.label: I prepared and planned ahead
I adapted when things shifted
The team came through for me
It just worked out somehow

nodes.text: When things got difficult today, what was your first instinct?

options.label: Figure out what I could control
Wait for someone else to step in
Push through alone
Feel overwhelmed and stuck

nodes.text: Think of one specific decision you made today. How did you handle it?

options.label: Took full ownership of the outcome
Adjusted my approach when I saw it wasn't working
Waited to see what others would do first
Let someone else take the lead

nodes.text: Looking back at the hardest moment today — were there choices you had?

options.label: Yes — I could have acted differently
Yes — I could have asked for help sooner
Not really — circumstances decided it
I didn't stop to think about it

nodes.comment: Routes based on accumulated axis1 signals across the two questions

nodes.text:
You stayed in the driver's seat today — even when things were uncertain.

Noticing that you chose '{A1_Q2}' is not small. Agency isn't about controlling outcomes. It's about owning your responses. You did that.

Some days pull your attention outward — to what others did or didn't do, to circumstances beyond your reach. That's real, and it's human.

But somewhere today, even quietly, you made a call. The fact that you're here reflecting on it? That's already a sign of agency.
Now let's shift from how you handled things — to what you gave today.
Think about your interactions today. What stands out most?

options.label: I helped someone who needed it
I went beyond what was asked of me
I expected recognition I didn't get
I felt others weren't pulling their weight

nodes.text: In your most important interaction today, what was driving you underneath?

options.label: I wanted the other person to succeed
I wanted to improve the outcome for everyone
I wanted credit for what I'd done
I wanted others to notice my effort

nodes.text: Was there a moment today when you did something helpful without being asked?

options.label: Yes — and I didn't expect anything in return
Yes — and I hoped someone would notice
Not really — I stayed in my lane
I looked for opportunities but didn't act

nodes.comment: Routes based on accumulated axis2 signals

nodes.text:
Today, you gave more than the minimum.

'{A2_OPEN}' — that's the kind of action that quietly shapes a team's culture. The people who shift how teams feel are rarely the loudest ones in the room. They're the ones who act before they're asked.

There's nothing wrong with wanting recognition — effort deserves acknowledgment.

But when the expectation of reward shapes the action, the action gets smaller. Worth sitting with: what would you have done today if no one were watching and no credit were coming?
We've looked at how you acted, and what you gave. Now let's zoom out — beyond yourself.
When you think about today's biggest challenge — who comes to mind?

options.label: Mostly just me and my work
My team — we were all in it together
A specific colleague who had it harder than me
The customer or end user we're serving

nodes.text: How did your work today land for people beyond yourself?

options.label: It mostly affected my own deliverables
It moved something forward for the team
It directly helped a colleague or user
It created value for people I may never meet

nodes.text: Did you check in on someone today — not for a task, but just as a person?

options.label: Yes — and it felt genuine
Yes — quickly, mostly out of habit
No — I was too focused on my own work
No — but I noticed someone who could have used it

nodes.comment: Routes based on accumulated axis3 signals

nodes.text:
Today your focus was tight — on your own work, your own outcomes. That's not a flaw; focus matters.

But the most durable source of meaning at work isn't personal achievement. It's realizing your work matters to someone else. Tomorrow: look for one person your work touches.

You thought beyond yourself today. You saw '{A3_OPEN}' as part of the challenge — not just your own deliverable.

That's perspective-taking in action. It's also, quietly, the antidote to a lot of workplace suffering. The wider your concern, the less any single moment can shrink you.

That's it for today.

You showed up, you reflected — and that's already more than most. See you tomorrow.
